coredata.dc:title: Photo-induced electron transfer between carbon dots and menadione in non-ionic micelles: A triplet states study with magnetic field
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar Behera, Ranjan
Mishra, Leepsa
Panigrahi, Aradhana
Dubey, Priyanka
Kumar Sarangi, Manas
coredata.dc:description: Compared to homogeneous surroundings, the kinetics of ultrafast photo-induced processes gets perturbed in self-assemblies. Herein, we attempt to unveil the photophysical alterations in highly luminescent carbon dots (CDs) with menadione (MQ-Vitamin K3), in homogeneous aqueous medium and nonionic Brij-35 self-assemblies. With a series of steady-state and time-resolved studies, we report photoinduced electron transfer between the CDs and MQ for Brij-35. Host-guest-like encapsulation in micelles can severely deter the diffusion dynamics of the transient intermediates. With an external magnetic field, we trace an enhancement in the radical products with controlled diffusion and spin dynamics for Brij-35 than the aqueous surrounding.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Menadione (MQ)
Brij-35
Charge transfer
Magnetic field effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The rate of quenching Equation: The Stern-Volmer (SV) equations are used to calculate the rate of quenching from the following relation [38]

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the Science and Engineering Research Board, Govt. of India, for a Research grant under sanction CRG/2022/004870 and RA fellowship to LM. All the authors thank Prof. Samita Basu, SINP Kolkata, for many fruitful and brainstorming discussions. AP acknowledges the fellowship from IIT Patna. PD acknowledges the Ministry of Education through Prime Minister's Research Fellowship (PMRF) funding (PMRF-ID: 2702445).

The authors acknowledge the Science and Engineering Research Board, Govt. of India, for a Research grant under sanction CRG/2022/004870 and RA fellowship to LM. All the authors thank Prof. Samita Basu, SINP Kolkata, for many fruitful and brainstorming discussions. AP acknowledges the fellowship from IIT Patna. PD acknowledges the Ministry of Education through Prime Minister's Research Fellowship (PMRF) funding (PMRF-ID: 2702445).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron micrographs (inset: size distributions) of CDs. (b) UV–Vis absorption (left), the excitation spectra at λem = 455 nm (middle) and emission spectra at λex = 370 nm (right), and (c) PL lifetime decay profiles at λex = 377 nm with λem = 455 nm of CDs (2 μg/mL) in water (orange) and Brij-35 (dark cyan). The black dotted line (c) is the instrument response function for the TCSPC setup. The fitted line for the lifetime decay is shown in red (water) and olive (Brij-35). Each decay is fitted with three exponentials, as given in Eq. (1). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

PL quenching measurements of CDs (2 μg/mL) in (a) water, (b) Brij-35 at λex = 370 nm, (c) F0/F vs. [MQ] plot, (d, e) the PL lifetime decay profile (λex = 377 nm) of CDs in water (d) and Brij-35 (e) media with the sequential addition of MQ (0.1––1 mM, an interval of 0.1 mM). (f) SV plots τ0/τ vs. [MQ] for the media are shown in the figure. Insets of Fig. 2a and b represent their respective UV visible absorption spectra for similar measurement conditions.

Triplet-triplet (T-T) transient absorption spectra generated after 1 µs of laser flash at λex = 355 nm for CDs (2 μg/mL) and MQ in (a) water (orange) and (b) Brij-35 (dark cyan). The pink and violet colors represent the spectra for 0.5 mM and 1 mM concentrations of MQ for (a) and (b). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

T-T transient absorption spectra, generated after 1 µs of laser flash at λex = 355 nm for CDs (2 μg/mL) and MQ (1 mM) in (a) water and (b) Brij-35. The violet and magenta curves represent the spectra without and with MF (0.08 T), respectively. The inset figures show their corresponding decay profiles at 370 nm wavelength. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Upper panels a, and b represent the transient absorption spectra of CDs in water and Brij-35 for time delay from 0.5 to 4.9 μs generated after the laser flash photolysis respectively. Similar time-delayed spectra in the presence of MF are shown in c (water) and d (Brij-35).
A schematic representation of the reaction pathways for the photoinduced interactions between CDs and MQ in water and Brij-35 micelles.

head.ce:title: Photo-induced electron transfer between carbon dots and menadione in non-ionic micelles: A triplet states study with magnetic field

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, 222/IV, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, 801106, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, 222/IV, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, 801106, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Compared to homogeneous surroundings, the kinetics of ultrafast photo-induced processes gets perturbed in self-assemblies. Herein, we attempt to unveil the photophysical alterations in highly luminescent carbon dots (CDs) with menadione (MQ-Vitamin K3), in homogeneous aqueous medium and nonionic Brij-35 self-assemblies. With a series of steady-state and time-resolved studies, we report photoinduced electron transfer between the CDs and MQ for Brij-35. Host-guest-like encapsulation in micelles can severely deter the diffusion dynamics of the transient intermediates. With an external magnetic field, we trace an enhancement in the radical products with controlled diffusion and spin dynamics for Brij-35 than the aqueous surrounding.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datas are provided in the manuscript and SM

ce:section.ce:para: Self-organized nano-assemblies are treated as a pseudo model for simple biological mimetic systems and possess the properties of the immediate local environment of “biological water” [1]. Interfacial ultrafast processes like sum-frequency generation, photoinduced charge transfer (PICT), etc., in such assemblies, have been the focus of intense research in recent times [2]. Various photophysical properties like dielectric relaxation, hydrogen bonding, solvation, electrical conductivity, and solubility get affected in the neighborhood of such a medium which gives rise to new characteristic features [3–6]. The micelles have several advantages over a homogeneous medium because of a super-cage-like environment and differential hydrophilic and hydrophobic compartments. It can incorporate the solute molecules with adequate dimension and can control the diffusion dynamics of the intermediates resulting from various interfacial reaction kinetics [7–9]. The non-ionic micelles exhibit slow relaxation because of the absence of ionic repulsion between the head groups [4]. The disparity in the solubility with the presence of both hydrophilic and hydrophobic surfaces modulates the electronic transition states and hence, influences the optical properties of the fluorophore compared to the homogeneous environment.

Carbon-based nanoparticles with sizes smaller than 10 nm are of growing interest in the scientific community due to their tunable optical band gap, superior quantum yield, water-solubility, less toxicity, and low-cost synthesis [10–12]. They are widely used for sensing, optoelectronic devices, catalysis, imaging, LEDs, photovoltaic devices, etc. [13–17]. The core structures of carbon dots (CDs) are highly complex and show size-tunable photoluminescence (PL) [10] and excitation-dependent PL (EDPL) [18,19]. Several hypotheses, such as quantum confinement, the presence of molecular trap states, surface states [20,21], multiple emissive states [22], and extended π-conjugations [23], etc., have been employed to explain the cause of such PL. Surface treatments and doping in CDs have also been reported to modulate such EDPL behavior [24,25]. One of the interesting outcomes that brought considerable attention is the tunable photo-induced charge transport and transfer behavior in such modified CDs [26–28]. In this article, we address the photophysics of CDs in non-ionic Brij-35 micelles and compare their optical behavior to that of a homogeneous aqueous medium. As both the media are neutral, the impact of microheterogeneity and the non-ionic bipolarity of Brij-35 on the photo-induced interactions of CDs with redox-active quinones are elucidated.

Organized assemblies like micelles with hydrophilic (interface) and hydrophobic (core) rich regions can encapsulate the interacting donor (D) and acceptor (A) molecules, forming a host–guest complex [29,30]. This increases the formation of the encounter complex and hence the charge transfer (CT). Micelles, due to their cage-like structure, can control the diffusion dynamics of the transient intermediates, enhancing the reaction dynamics [31–33]. CT in such assemblies can control the fast-diffusing intermediates and often prolong the product lifetime, making them easier to detect. As CT often generates products with unpaired electrons, the spin-correlation between the D and A transient radicals can be critically maintained by tuning the inter-radical distances in such micro assemblies. The spin-correlated geminate radical pairs with optimum separation distances and controlled diffusion are readily perturbed by the application of an external magnetic field (MF) [34–37]. MF of the order of hyperfine interaction (HFI) can lift the degeneracy of the D-A correlated triplet state by Zeeman splitting and hence severely disrupts the intersystem crossing (ISC). This results in a sudden increase in the population of the initially formed singlet (S) or triplet (T) radical intermediates. The increase in the transient population is an indication of the growth of the transient and evidence of the CT-induced pathways. Herein, to study the role of encapsulating interface, we report the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) between CDs and menadione (MQ) in the neutral Brij-35 micelles and have compared their results to that of the homogeneous aqueous medium. Studying the mechanistic pathways of these CT at the interfacial region remains an utmost priority, and controlling them through an external perturbation adds another dimension to its regulative dynamics. The outcomes of the manuscript indicate that just by altering the surrounding confined medium, we can elucidate the features of underlying reaction pathways. Moreover, with the application of an external MF, we can unveil the short-lived intermediates and kinetically alter and modulate the photo-induced processes.

Compounds with the high purity required for the synthesis of CDs (citric acid (CA) > 99.5 %), non-ionic micelles (Brij-35 > 99 %), and quinones (MQ > 99.5 %) are procured from Merck. All are of analytical grade and used as received without any further purifications. Triple distilled water is used for preparing all aqueous solutions. The Brij-35 micelles are prepared with a concentration of 10 % molar mass (well above the critical micellar concentration) in triple distilled water at pH-7.0.

The pyrolysis method is used to synthesize the CDs, as reported in earlier literature [24]. In brief, CA (1.0 g) is dissolved in 10 mL of triple distilled water with vigorous stirring at 500 rpm at 250 °C (130 °C in reaction medium) on a hot plate for 5 h. Triple distilled water is added regularly to the solution for the continuation of the reaction and to avoid any burning. Afterward, the solution is transformed into brownish gel-like stuff indicating the synthesis of CDs. The CDs are then purified by column chromatography using the reported protocol [24].

The UV–visible absorption and PL measurements are performed with a spectrophotometer (JASCO V-770 Japan) and Fluoromax-4 (Horiba) fluorimeter, respectively. All steady-state measurements are recorded at room temperature with neutral pH by Hellma Quartz cuvettes of 1 cm path length.

The excited state time-resolved spectroscopic profiles are recorded by using a modular time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) spectrometer (DeltaFlex, Horiba Scientific) having multichannel plate photmultiplier tube MCP-PMT as the detector. The emission is collected perpendicular to the direction of the excitation beam at a magic angle. Each decay is fitted with the discrete multi-exponential function (Eq. (1)) after de-convoluting the instrument response function (IRF) signal. An in-built program in the Ez-time data analyzer is used to fit the multiexponential decays, where the reduced χ2 value and the residuals are accounted for the goodness of the fit. The average lifetime is measured as per Eq. (2). Details of the setup are mentioned elsewhere [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The rate of quenching Equation: The Stern-Volmer (SV) equations are used to calculate the rate of quenching from the following relation [38]
ce:section.ce:para:
(3a)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
C
(3b)
<
τ 0
>
< τ >
= 1 +
k q
<
τ 0
>
C
Where
F 0
and F ,
τ 0
and τ are the PL intensities and lifetimes of CDs without and with the addition of quencher [C],
K
S V
and
k q
are the SV and bimolecular quenching constants, respectively.

A nanosecond flash photolysis system (Applied Photo-physics) comprising Nd: YAG (Lab series, model lab 150, Spectra-Physics) laser with third harmonic output (λex = 355 nm having full-width half maxima of 8 ns) is used to generate the triplet transients. Here, the pump laser excites the sample, which is in both temporal and spatial coherence with a pulsed xenon lamp (250 W). A photo-multiplier IP28 is used as the detector. The wavelength from the probe beam is fed into a Tektronix oscilloscope (TDS 3054B, 500 MHz, 5 Gs/sec), and is recorded and analyzed by an in-built TEKVISA software. More details of the setup are described elsewhere [39–41]. Each sample is degassed for a minimum of 20 mins with ultrapure argon gas before the experimentation. For MF measurement, a pair of DC electromagnetic coils are placed around the sample chamber with an external control to modulate the field. All measurements are done at room temperature with a specially designed quartz cuvette of 10 mm path length. Origin 8.5 and B-spline are used for plotting and tracing out the spectra.
(4)

=

;

R a d i c a l

Where B(t) is the change in the absorption with time t at a particular wavelength λ while B0 represents the amplitude of the transients. The rate constant
k
T
is the inverse of the lifetime of the molecule at the triplet excited state (
τ

). The

T
are the absorbance of the molecules in the triplet state without and with MF,
τ
MF
and

WMF
are the average lifetime of the transients in the presence and absence of MF.

The high-resolution image profiles are recorded using TEM with a JEOL-F200 operated at 200 kV. The samples are prepared by drop-casting over the carbon-coated copper grid having 300 mesh and dried overnight. Image J software is used for the analysis of the micrograph, and Origin 8.5 is used for plotting the size distribution histogram.

PerkinElmer spectrum 400 spectrometer is used to trace the FTIR spectra of the CDs in attenuated total reflection mode and is scanned over the wavenumber range 500–4000 cm−1. Litesizer 500 of Anton Paar instrument with irradiation wavelength 660 nm is used to record the Zeta potential (ζ- potential) at room temperature.

Fig. 1 a represents the TEM micrograph of the synthesized CDs, which appears spherical with an average diameter of ∼ 4 nm as obtained from the histogram (inset of Fig. 1a). Fig. 1b and c reflect the steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopic characterization of CDs for the aqueous (orange) and Brij-35 (dark cyan) micelles, respectively. The characteristic absorption spectra of CDs in both environments (Fig. 1b) show two types of electronic transitions, one sharp peak (∼200 nm) due to the carbogenic core (π -π* transition) and the second with a broader absorption (∼280–300 nm) for n-π* transition for the molecular state [42]. Apart from that, a weak absorption tail of ∼ 400 nm is attributed to the surface state transition in CDs. To further characterize their excited state optical behavior, emission, and excitation profiles are recorded at λex = 370 nm and λem = 455 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b). We observe almost overlapping excitation and emission spectra for both the media. This indicates that the CDs in the micellar medium preferably remain in the hydrophilic region of Brij-35 and have spectral properties similar to a homogeneous aqueous environment. To comprehend the impact of the interfacial interactions and the influence of the surface state of CDs on EDPL, the emission profile is recorded at different excitation wavelengths starting from 280 nm to 440 nm in both media (Fig. S1). A similar excitation dependency in the two medium is in line with the earlier observations. To better understand their photo-excited behavior, we have recorded their emission lifetime with TCSPC (Fig. 1c). With excitation at λex = 377 nm, the two media illustrate a similar photo relaxation pathway reflecting almost overlapping decay profiles, with a calculated average lifetime of 4.79 ns and 4.93 ns for water and Brij-35, respectively (Table ST1 and ST2). The closeness in the spectroscopic studies reflects that the CDs are facing an aqueous environment in Brij-35 and reside in the aqueous or interfacial region of the micelles. The surface of CDs often has dangling carboxylic acid and carbonyl groups which can freely take part in PET to the nearby molecule [24]. We have verified the surface charge and the dangling bonds with ζ-potential and FTIR measurements (Fig. S2a, b) respectively. The calculated value of ζ-potential (-7.48 mV) indicates that the surfaces are embedded with negatively charged species. This is supported by the presence of a carbonyl group at 1638 cm−1 and carboxylic acid at ∼ 3300 cm−1 in the FTIR measurement.

It is reported that the micro-heterogeneity and the nature of the surface entity play important roles in PET processes [27,43]. The motivation behind this study is to investigate the role of such non-ionic micro-confinement in the CT between the CDs and MQ. It is noteworthy to mention here that selecting MQ as a quencher stems from two important considerations, i) it is an excellent electron acceptor, ii) due to its non-fluorescent nature, it does not interfere with the luminescence from the emitting fluorophore in the excited state. A typical absorption and emission profile of 1 mM MQ is shown in Fig. S2c for control studies. With both steady-state and time-resolved PL quenching with MQ, we have attempted to decipher the nature of CT in the respective media (Fig. 2 ). With the sequential addition of MQ from 0 to 1 mM, PL intensity is quenched in both water and Brij-35 media (Fig. 2a and b). Along with the quenching, we observe a slight blue shift for the Brij medium with the increase in MQ, substantiating that the interaction tends to be hydrophobic. The quenching rates (Fig. 2c) indicate that the interaction between CDs and MQ is dominant in the Brij-35 medium compared to that of water. Nonlinearity (upward) in SVplot reflects the possibility of a ground state complex formation between the reacting molecules and an associated PET among the two. The absorption spectra (inset Fig. 2a and b) reflect that both the transition states π-π* and n-π* for the CDs are influenced by MQ, reflecting possible ground state interactions with an isosbestic point at 285 nm. For more details on the PET, we have performed PL lifetime analysis in the presence of MQ (Fig. 2d and e). The decay profiles of CDs with MQ remain unchanged in the water medium; however, their average lifetime drops from 4.93 ns to 3.61 ns for Brij-35 (Table ST1 and ST2). PL quenching may be of two types, static and dynamic. While the ground state complex formation often leads to static quenching, dynamic quenching happens due to the collision of the particles leading to PET. To evaluate the rate of quenching, we have further represented SV plots using Eq. (3) (Fig. 2f). The quenching rate characterizes a possible PET between CDs and MQ for the Brij-35, while almost a flat line reflects a negligible PET for water.

To confirm the presence of PET between CDs and MQ, we have performed the same transient measurements in the presence of an external MF. PICT reactions like electron or proton transfer can generate geminate radicals or radical ion pairs having unpaired electrons. Encapsulated structures like micelles and reverse micelles can help in maintaining the spin correlation between the confined radical pairs (RPs) /radical ion pairs (RIPs) with an adequate separation distance between the pairs and hence restricts the free diffusion of photogenerated radical intermediates. Such geminate RPs/RIPs become vulnerable to the application of external MF. MF of the order of HFI can lift the degeneracy of the T states (T+, T0, T-) and can eventually restrict the ISC between the S and T, only allowing transition between S and T0. This increases the population and lifetimes of the RPs/RIPs formed in their respective photogenerated states. Fig. 4 a and b represent the T-T spectra of the CDs-MQ complex with and without MF for water and Brij-35 micelles, respectively. The spectra for the complex in the water medium remain almost unaltered in the presence of MF, representing almost no impact of the MF on the photogenerated products. This is evident from the overlaying spectral behavior and their decay kinetics (inset Fig. 4a, Fig. S3). On the other hand, in confinement, we observe distinct increments in the absorption both at 370 nm and 420 nm, indicating the growth of the triplet intermediate radicals. This is well supported by the increase in the decay kinetics of the radicals, as shown in the insets of Fig. 4 (Eqs. (4)–(6), and Table ST3).

Transient absorption spectra for the confined and aqueous medium in the presence and absence of MF at different time delays have been plotted in Fig. 5 . For the aqueous medium, with a gradual increment in the delay time from 0.5 to 5 μs, we hardly find any change in the spectra other than an obvious drop at 370 nm for MQ. The spectra remain almost overlapping with or without MF (Fig. 5a, c). This indicates that the application of MF has nearly no alteration to the radical quantum yield in the homogeneous aqueous media, similar to the earlier discussion indicating fast diffusion of the RPs/RIPs. On the other hand, the spectral characteristics in the Brij-35 show a clear demarcation with the increment in time delay from 0.5 to 5 μs, with distinct features for the RPs at 370 and 420 nm even after 5 μs of the laser pulse. The distinct spectral features remain almost intact even in the presence of MF (Fig. 5b, d). This indicates that PET and its transient intermediates are reasonably traceable, and time evolutions of the species could be characterized inside the confined micellar medium with MF. This gives an edge for unveiling the underlying pathways, as the fast diffusion of the RPs/RIPs is restricted in the organized assemblies.

In summary, we report some distinct changes in the photophysical behavior of the CDs with MQ in two neutral homogeneous aqueous and microheterogeneous Brij-35 environments. Encapsulating reactants not just regulate their random diffusion but also eases the CT processes. We have observed the transient PET intermediate RPs/RIPs and traced the reaction pathway inside the micelles in the triplet states with the application of external MF. The application of MF plays a diverse role with increased absorbance of the short-lived transients formed during PET along with higher radical quantum yield. While free ion formation is prevalent in the homogenous aqueous media due to the easy diffusion of the ion pairs, the MFE with geminate radical formation dominates in the Brij-35 organized assemblies. The microemulsion in the vicinity of CDs and encapsulation of MQ within Brij-35 significantly tempers the CT kinetics, which can help in the rational design of CT-based processes.

Ranjan Kumar Behera: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Leepsa Mishra: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Aradhana Panigrahi: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Resources. Priyanka Dubey: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Manas Kumar Sarangi: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Science and Engineering Research Board, Govt. of India, for a Research grant under sanction CRG/2022/004870 and RA fellowship to LM. All the authors thank Prof. Samita Basu, SINP Kolkata, for many fruitful and brainstorming discussions. AP acknowledges the fellowship from IIT Patna. PD acknowledges the Ministry of Education through Prime Minister's Research Fellowship (PMRF) funding (PMRF-ID: 2702445).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2023.140904.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
